---
title: "Oscars_Final_Project"
author: "Michael Overman"
date: "2026-05-04"
output: pdf_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

## R Markdown

This is an R Markdown document. Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents. For more details on using R Markdown see <http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com>.

When you click the **Knit** button a document will be generated that includes both content as well as the output of any embedded R code chunks within the document. You can embed an R code chunk like this:

```{r cars}
summary(cars)
```

## Including Plots

You can also embed plots, for example:

```{r pressure, echo=FALSE}
plot(pressure)
```

Note that the `echo = FALSE` parameter was added to the code chunk to prevent printing of the R code that generated the plot.

## Data Introduction

The dataset we chose to explore is the set of Oscar winners of Best Actor and
Best Actress from 1929 to 2018. The dataset comes from the Journal of Statistical Education, http://jse.amstat.org/datasets/oscars.dat.txt, updated through 2019 using information from Oscars.org and Wikipedia.org.

Each row of the dataset states the best actor/ actress from 1929-2018.If there is a tie, that makes there an extra winner for that year. This is essentially a full population of the winners for the best actor/actress in the Oscars, but it is also a sample because it doesn't go into all the awards that could be given out in the Oscars.

The full dataset contains 182 observations of 10 variables. Our report is concerned with 7 of
these variables for each observation: 
1. age - a numerical, discrete variable.
2. oscar_yr - the year the Oscar was awarded. This is a numerical(nominal?), discrete variable.
3. birth_y - the year the award-winning actor/actress was born. This is a nominal, discrete variable.
4. award - either 'Best Actor' or 'Best Actress'. This is a categorical variable.
5. birth_pl - the location the actor/actress was born. This is a categorical variable.
6. movie - the film the actor/actress won their Oscar for. This is a categorical variable.
7. name - the name of the award-winning actor/actress. This is a categorical variable.

## Research Questions & Hypotheses

Our research question is this: Is there a difference in average age between 'Best Actor' and
'Best Actress', and does this change from the period 1929-1974 to the period 1975-2018?

Our null hypothesis: There is no difference in average age between 'Best Actor' and 'Best
Actress', and this does not change from the period 1929-1974 to the period 1975-2018.

Our alternative hypothesis: There is a difference in average age between 'Best Actor' and
'Best Actress', and this difference changes from the period 1929-1974 to the period 1975-2018.

## Data Summary & Visualization

## Data Analysis

## Conclusion
